sources.citation: Booth, J. A., & Seligson, M. A. (2009). The Legitimacy Puzzle in Latin America: Political Support and Democracy in Eight Nations. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Directly theorizes and measures democratic legitimacy as a multidimensional construct encompassing diffuse and specific support across Latin American countries including Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Democratic legitimacy in Latin America is multidimensional, with citizens distinguishing between support for democratic principles and satisfaction with democratic performance.

sources.citation: Moreno, A. (2002). La sociedad mexicana y el cambio: Apoyo a la democracia. In A. Moreno (Ed.), El votante mexicano: Democracia, actitudes políticas y conducta electoral. Fondo de Cultura Económica.
sources.relevance: Analyzes Mexican citizens' normative commitment to democracy and satisfaction with its functioning in the context of democratic transition.
sources.key_finding: Mexican support for democracy increased significantly after the 2000 transition but remained tempered by dissatisfaction with democratic performance and institutional quality.

sources.citation: Norris, P. (2011). Democratic Deficit: Critical Citizens Revisited. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework of 'critical citizens' who support democratic ideals but are dissatisfied with democratic practice, directly applicable to the normative vs. evaluative dimensions captured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Growing gaps between democratic aspirations and perceived democratic performance generate 'critical citizens' who demand better governance rather than reject democracy outright.

sources.citation: Crow, D. (2010). The Party's Over: Citizen Conceptions of Democracy and Political Dissatisfaction in Mexico. Comparative Politics, 43(1), 41–61.
sources.relevance: Examines how Mexican citizens conceptualize democracy and how these conceptions shape satisfaction with democratic governance.
sources.key_finding: Citizens who define democracy in substantive terms (economic well-being, social justice) are significantly less satisfied with Mexican democracy than those who define it procedurally.

sources.citation: Latinobarómetro. (2018). Informe 2018. Corporación Latinobarómetro.
sources.relevance: Provides comparative survey data on democratic support and satisfaction across Latin America, using items closely paralleling those in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Support for democracy in Mexico declined to historic lows by 2018, with only 38% of Mexicans stating democracy is preferable to any other form of government.

sources.citation: Schedler, A. (2012). The Politics of Uncertainty: Sustaining and Subverting Electoral Authoritarianism. Oxford University Press.
sources.relevance: Theorizes how citizen confidence in electoral integrity is central to democratic consolidation in Mexico, directly relevant to the INE-related items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Electoral confidence in Mexico is shaped by partisan cues, media exposure, and direct experience with electoral irregularities, and is critical for regime legitimacy.

sources.citation: Moreno, A., & Méndez, P. (2007). La confianza institucional en México. In A. Moreno (Ed.), Nuestros valores: Los mexicanos en México y en Estados Unidos al inicio del siglo XXI. Banamex.
sources.relevance: Directly measures institutional trust in Mexico across multiple institutions including police, electoral authorities, and interpersonal trust.
sources.key_finding: Institutional trust in Mexico is consistently low, with police and political parties ranking among the least trusted institutions, while interpersonal trust also remains below regional averages.

sources.citation: Eisenstadt, T. A. (2004). Courting Democracy in Mexico: Party Strategies and Electoral Institutions. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the evolution of electoral institutions in Mexico and how citizen confidence in the IFE/INE shaped democratic transition.
sources.key_finding: The credibility of Mexico's electoral management body was pivotal to democratic transition, but confidence eroded as partisan conflicts over electoral reform intensified.

sources.citation: Córdova, L. (2008). La reforma electoral y el cambio político en México. In D. Zovatto & J. J. Orozco (Eds.), Reforma política y electoral en América Latina 1978–2007. UNAM/IDEA Internacional.
sources.relevance: Discusses how electoral reforms and the role of INE in overseeing state elections affect citizen confidence in electoral processes.
sources.key_finding: Centralization of electoral oversight under INE was designed to increase confidence in subnational elections, but results have been mixed depending on local political dynamics.

sources.citation: Levi, M., & Stoker, L. (2000). Political Trust and Trustworthiness. Annual Review of Political Science, 3(1), 475–507.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational theoretical framework for understanding political trust as distinct from interpersonal trust, relevant to the construct's combination of institutional and social trust items.
sources.key_finding: Political trust is generated by institutional performance and procedural fairness, and is conceptually and empirically distinct from generalized social trust.

sources.citation: Campbell, A., Gurin, G., & Miller, W. E. (1954). The Voter Decides. Row, Peterson and Company.
sources.relevance: Foundational work that introduced the concept of political efficacy, distinguishing internal efficacy (belief in one's competence) from external efficacy (belief in government responsiveness), both captured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Political efficacy is a key predictor of political participation, with citizens who feel efficacious being significantly more likely to vote and engage in political activities.

sources.citation: Moreno, A. (2003). El votante mexicano: Democracia, actitudes políticas y conducta electoral. Fondo de Cultura Económica.
sources.relevance: Analyzes political interest, efficacy, and engagement among Mexican voters, using items closely paralleling those in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Political efficacy in Mexico is stratified by education and socioeconomic status, with lower-income citizens reporting significantly lower internal and external efficacy.

sources.citation: Klesner, J. L. (2007). Social Capital and Political Participation in Latin America: Evidence from Argentina, Chile, Mexico, and Peru. Latin American Research Review, 42(2), 1–32.
sources.relevance: Examines the relationship between social capital, political efficacy, and various forms of political participation in Mexico and other Latin American countries.
sources.key_finding: Social capital and political efficacy independently predict political participation in Mexico, but their effects vary across conventional and unconventional participation modes.

sources.citation: Buendía, J., & Somuano, F. (2003). Participación electoral en nuevas democracias: La elección presidencial de 2000 en México. Política y Gobierno, 10(2), 289–323.
sources.relevance: Directly examines how political interest and perceived efficacy shaped electoral participation during Mexico's democratic transition.
sources.key_finding: Political interest and external efficacy were significant predictors of voter turnout in the 2000 Mexican presidential election, even after controlling for sociodemographic variables.

sources.citation: Seligson, M. A., & Booth, J. A. (2009). Predicting Coups? Democratic Values and Authoritarian Attitudes among Elites and the General Public in Latin America. Comparative Political Studies, 42(10), 1–28.
sources.relevance: Directly measures authoritarian attitudes in Latin America, including preference for strong leaders over rule of law, which is central to this construct.
sources.key_finding: Authoritarian predispositions among Latin American publics are significantly associated with lower education, lower political knowledge, and experience with insecurity.

sources.citation: Moreno, A. (2005). Nuestros valores: Los mexicanos en México y en Estados Unidos al inicio del siglo XXI. Banamex.
sources.relevance: Examines Mexican value orientations including the freedom-security tradeoff and tolerance for strong leadership, directly paralleling items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: A substantial proportion of Mexicans prioritize security over freedom and express tolerance for rule-bending by authorities, particularly in contexts of high insecurity.

sources.citation: Canache, D. (2012). Citizens' Conceptualizations of Democracy: Structural Complexity, Substantive Content, and Political Significance. Comparative Political Studies, 45(9), 1132–1158.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how citizens' conceptualizations of democracy relate to authoritarian predispositions, relevant to the tension between democratic norms and authoritarian preferences.
sources.key_finding: Citizens with thin or instrumental conceptions of democracy are more likely to endorse authoritarian alternatives when democratic performance is perceived as poor.

sources.citation: Feldman, S. (2003). Enforcing Social Conformity: A Theory of Authoritarianism. Political Psychology, 24(1), 41–74.
sources.relevance: Provides the psychological theory of authoritarianism as a predisposition activated by threat, relevant to the security-freedom tradeoff and rule-breaking tolerance items.
sources.key_finding: Authoritarian predispositions are activated by perceived threats to social order, leading to increased support for strong leaders and willingness to sacrifice civil liberties.

sources.citation: Romero, V. (2016). Desempeño gubernamental y actitudes hacia la democracia en México. Política y Gobierno, 23(2), 409–441.
sources.relevance: Examines how government performance evaluations in Mexico relate to authoritarian attitudes and democratic backsliding preferences.
sources.key_finding: Poor government performance evaluations increase authoritarian predispositions among Mexican citizens, particularly among those with lower education and greater economic vulnerability.

sources.citation: Hagopian, F. (1998). Democracy and Political Representation in Latin America in the 1990s: Pause, Reorganization, or Decline? In F. Agüero & J. Stark (Eds.), Fault Lines of Democracy in Post-Transition Latin America (pp. 99–143). North-South Center Press.
sources.relevance: Discusses the perceived power of various political actors—parties, business, unions, the Church—in shaping Latin American democracies, directly relevant to the actors listed in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Citizens' perceptions of which actors wield political influence shape their evaluations of democratic quality and their sense of political exclusion.

sources.citation: Lawson, C. (2002). Building the Fourth Estate: Democratization and the Rise of a Free Press in Mexico. University of California Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the role of media as a political actor in Mexico's democratization, relevant to the perceived influence of media item in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Media became an increasingly autonomous and influential political actor during Mexico's democratic transition, and citizens' perceptions of media power reflect broader assessments of democratic pluralism.

sources.citation: Lehoucq, F. (2012). The Politics of Modern Central America: Civil War, Democratization, and Underdevelopment. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides comparative context on how perceptions of elite capture (business, church, military) undermine democratic legitimacy in Latin America.
sources.key_finding: When citizens perceive that economic elites and non-democratic actors dominate politics, democratic satisfaction and institutional trust decline significantly.

sources.citation: Camp, R. A. (2014). Politics in Mexico: Democratic Consolidation or Decline? (6th ed.). Oxford University Press.
sources.relevance: Comprehensive analysis of power structures in Mexican politics, including the relative influence of the presidency, parties, business, unions, the Church, and civil society.
sources.key_finding: The Mexican presidency remains perceived as the most influential political actor, but the growing influence of business groups and media has reshaped citizens' mental maps of political power.

gap_justification: While the individual actors (president, parties, media, etc.) have been studied extensively in Mexican politics, the specific construct of citizens' comparative perceptions of influence across multiple actor types measured simultaneously is less common in the academic literature. Most studies focus on one actor at a time (e.g., media influence, business power) rather than examining the full ecology of perceived influence. This construct fills an important gap by capturing how citizens mentally map the distribution of political power, which is essential for understanding whether they perceive democracy as pluralistic or captured by specific interests. In the Mexican context, where power has historically been concentrated in the presidency and ruling party, tracking shifts in perceived influence across actors provides a unique barometer of democratic consolidation or regression.
